classification_note: Fourth verification path (Python ref / Python minimal / Rust / Go). All four owned by Wise.Est Systems. The external-validation gap is NOT closed by this track. Per docs/release/THIRD_PARTY_VERIFIER_BRIEF_v0.1.md §8, a verifier authored, owned, and operated by a non-first-party party is required.

covered_behaviors: produces deterministic output across two runs
does not import or shell out to Python or the Rust verifier

makefile_targets_added.reason: WORK ORDER 013 — do not add to make ci until cold confirmation in a future work order

files_modified: Makefile (added .PHONY entries + go-verifier-check + go-verifier-fingerprints recipes; NOT added to ci)
known_limitations: Same-repo, same-owner. The Go track is logically independent (different language, different module surface, stdlib-only) but organizationally first-party. This does NOT constitute third-party validation per docs/release/IMPLEMENTATION_TRACKER.md §1.

Canonicalization parity sidesteps Python repr(float) divergence by using encoding/json with json.Decoder.UseNumber(); number literals are pass-through. This is strictly correct for the v0.1.0 corpus and for any JSON number the parser accepts, but it does not exercise Go's float-formatting code path. A different canonicalization scheme that required emitting numbers in normalized form would need separate work.

Go's default JSON encoder escapes <, >, & to \u003c / \u003e / \u0026. The implementation explicitly disables this via json.Encoder.SetEscapeHTML(false) — but no committed corpus entry contains <, >, or & inside a string. If a future corpus entry adds one, parity remains correct due to the disabled escaping, but the corpus does not currently test that path.
The CLI locates the repo root by walking up from cwd until it finds vectors/ and canonicalization/ as sibling directories. It does not accept a --repo-root flag yet.
The Go verifier is intentionally NOT yet wired into make ci, per WORK ORDER 013.
largest_remaining_external_validation_ga: A verifier authored, owned, and operated by a party other than Wise.Est Systems. Four first-party tracks (Python reference, Python minimal, Rust, Go) now all reproduce the three frozen fingerprints from independent code paths. This closes 'is the spec re-implementable across languages?' very strongly. It does not close the ownership / accountability question that defines third-party validation in docs/release/IMPLEMENTATION_TRACKER.md §1 and docs/release/THIRD_PARTY_VERIFIER_BRIEF_v0.1.md.
next_build_task: Either (a) promote the Go verifier into make ci after one more cold confirmation run, or (b) post the recruitment pitch (already drafted in docs/release/THIRD_PARTY_VERIFIER_BRIEF_v0.1.md and EXTERNAL_REVIEW_PACKET_v0.1.md) to a non-first-party audience and wait for an external submission. (a) tightens the in-repo gate; (b) is the only path that closes the gap.
